preamble: The shift from an alveolar or dental stop [d] to a retroflex stop (like [ɖ] or [ʈ]) typically occurs when the consonant is influenced by an adjacent rhotic sound [r] or through specific historical substrate influences. The tongue tip curls back further into the mouth.

examples.note: Proto-Dravidian *ḍ is a reconstructed retroflex stop. Tamil ட between vowels is voiced [ɖ]; e.g., 'aṭai' (room) shows retroflex [ɖ]. Kannada 'haḍa' (bald) shows ḍ contrasting with 'd'.
Supradental/retroflex assimilation: /r/ + /d/ cluster merges into a single voiced retroflex stop [ɖ] in connected speech.
RUKI/sibilant-triggered retroflexion: *sd → Sanskrit ḍ (voiced retroflex stop).

sources: Fortson, B. W. (2010). Indo-European Language and Culture: An Introduction (2nd ed.). Wiley-Blackwell.
